1: ēnoixen (aorist) — the Lamb's opening of each seal is a distinct completed act, not a continuous process. The Lamb (arnion) is consistently the grammatical subject of the seal-openings throughout Revelation 6, placing his agency at the center of the judgment sequence. The living creature's command "Come!" (erchou) is addressed to the horsemen, not to John — imperative, summoning the next judgment into the historical arena.
2: stephanos — a victor's laurel wreath (as in athletic contests), not diadema (the royal crown worn by sovereigns). The distinction matters: the white rider's crown is a conqueror's prize, earned in battle, not sovereign authority by nature. toxon (bow) — no arrow is mentioned; the bow without an arrow may signal the threat of conquest without its immediate execution. nikōn kai hina nikēsē — a present participle ("conquering") followed by an aorist purpose-clause ("in order that he might conquer"): ongoing conquest aimed at total victory.
3: The seal-opening formula repeats six times in Revelation 6 with minor variations (hote ēnoixen, "when he opened"). The formulaic repetition creates a judicial rhythm: the Lamb presides, the living creatures summon, the rider executes. The repetition is itself a rhetorical signal of divine control over the sequence — history unfolds in measured acts, not chaos.
4: pyrros (fiery red) — the color of blood and fire; Zechariah 1:8 has a red horse among the patrol horses. machaira — the short sword associated with assassination and civil violence, as opposed to rhomphaia (the large sword of sovereign/divine judgment in Revelation 1:16; Revelation 2:12). The distinction: the second rider wields the instrument of internal strife and murder, not conquest in formal warfare. sphaxōsin (slay one another) — the reflexive-intensive use: mutual slaughter, not external invasion.
5: zygos (pair of scales) — the measurement instrument for rationing; the black horse rider measures out grain, signaling not absolute famine but severe scarcity. Black (melas) in Lamentations 5:10 is the color of skin blackened by famine. The scales activate the economic register of the judgment: controlled distribution under crisis conditions.
6: choinix sitou dēnariou — a choinix of wheat (roughly 1 liter, a day's ration for one person) costs a dēnarion (a day's wage, Matthew 20:2). Normal prices in antiquity were 8-12 choinikes per dēnarion, so this represents roughly an 8-12x price increase — severe inflation but not total starvation. mē adikēsēs (do not harm — aorist subjunctive of prohibition) — the oil and wine (luxury goods) are exempted, suggesting the judgment falls hardest on those dependent on grain (the poor) while the wealthy continue relatively unaffected.
7: The fourth seal formula is identical to the second and third. John carefully maintains the structural symmetry: the progression from white (conquest) → red (civil war) → black (famine) → pale (death) traces an escalating logic of historical judgment.
8: chloros — literally green, the sickly pale color of disease and decay; the only use of chloros for a horse; elsewhere it describes vegetation (grass, Revelation 8:7; Revelation 9:4). The pallor of a corpse. Thanatos (Death personified) and Hadēs (the underworld realm) are paired — Death kills, Hades receives. rhomphaia, limos, thanatos, thēria (sword, famine, pestilence/death, wild beasts) — the four agents echo Ezekiel 14:21 exactly (YHWH's four severe judgments on Jerusalem: sword, famine, wild beasts, plague). John alludes to the Ezekiel formula with one substitution.
9: hypokatō tou thysiastēriou — "under the altar." In the Mosaic sacrificial system, the blood of the burnt offering was poured at the base (hypo) of the altar (Leviticus 4:7, 18). The martyrs are positioned as sacrificial blood — their deaths are the ultimate offering, their location at the altar's base making them liturgically present as sacrifices. hesphasmenōn (perfect passive participle of sphazō — slaughtered/killed; the same verb as "the Lamb as though slaughtered" in Revelation 5:6) — the martyrs share the vocabulary of the Lamb's own death.
10: despota — not Kyrie (covenant-Lord) but despotēs (absolute owner/master), used elsewhere for God in Luke 2:29; Acts 4:24; 2 Peter 2:1; Jude 4. The title of absolute sovereignty is invoked specifically in the context of the prayer for justice — the martyr-souls appeal to the one who owns them completely. ekdikeis (present tense: do you judge and avenge?) — an impatient present, not a future hope: "are you not judging?" tous katoikountas epi tēs gēs — "those who dwell on the earth" becomes Revelation's technical phrase for the world-system opposed to God (used 11 more times).
11: stolē leukē — a white robe (stolē, the long robe worn by priests and dignitaries; cf. Revelation 7:9,13,14). The gift of the robe signals vindication and priestly dignity. plērōthōsin (aorist passive subjunctive of plēroō) — "until the number might be completed/fulfilled." The martyrs' death-quota must be filled; this is not passive resignation but the framework that interprets ongoing persecution as purposive, not random. The present tense of the martyrdom-mandate ("those about to be killed") implies ongoing persecution at the time of writing.
12: seismos megas (great earthquake) — seismic language for divine theophany runs from Exodus 19:18 through Isaiah 29:6 and Joel 2:10 into Revelation's judgment vocabulary. sakkos trichinos (sackcloth made of hair) — the mourning garment; the blackening of the sun in mourning-cloth recalls Isaiah 50:3 ("I clothe the sky with darkness and make sackcloth its covering"). The cosmic signs of vv12-14 draw on the OT "Day of YHWH" tradition.
13: hōs sykē ballei (as a fig tree drops) — the fig tree's unripe winter fruit (olynthous, the late-hanging figs that fall in a winter gale) is the specific image. This precise detail (unripe figs falling off-season in a storm) is from Isaiah 34:4 (the fall of Edom's host of heaven). The Greek of Revelation reproduces the Isaianic imagery with unusual botanical specificity.
14: apechōristhē hōs biblion helissomenon — the sky split apart like a scroll being rolled up; Isaiah 34:4 again: "the sky will be rolled up like a scroll." Helissomenon (present passive participle — being rolled up, in process) gives the visual of a scroll being wound shut at both ends. ekinēthēsan (aorist passive) — every mountain and island was moved from its place; the world-map is being disassembled.
15: The sevenfold list of humanity (kings, great ones, generals, rich, powerful, every slave, every free person) is a merism for all of human social hierarchy — from top to bottom, no one is exempt. The syntax places the powerful first and the powerless last, then collapses the distinction: "every slave and every free person" — the great leveling of the Day of Judgment.
16: orgē tou arniou — "the wrath of the Lamb" — the most theologically paradoxical phrase in Revelation. The Lamb (arnion: diminutive, a small lamb) is the symbol of innocent meekness and sacrificial death; yet his orgē (wrath) is the most terrifying prospect facing the world-system's inhabitants. The phrase is unique; nowhere else in the NT is divine wrath attributed specifically to Christ as the Lamb.
17: ēlthen (aorist: has come, arrived) — not "is coming" but "has arrived." The great day of wrath is treated as already present, not still future. tis dynatai stathēnai — "who is able to stand?" — an OT formula for surviving divine judgment (Psalms 76:7; Malachi 3:2: "who can endure the day of his coming? who can stand when he appears?"). The rhetorical question anticipates Revelation 7's answer: the sealed servants of God.
